title: Avial

overview.description: A thick South Indian vegetable dish made with mixed vegetables, coconut, green chilli and yogurt, finished with curry leaves and coconut oil.

ingredients: 150g green beans, cut into short lengths
1 raw banana, peeled and cut into batons, optional
method: Add the grated coconut, green chillies, cumin seeds and water to a blender.
Blend to a coarse paste and set aside.
Place the carrots, green beans, butternut squash, swede and raw banana if using in a large saucepan.
Add the water, turmeric and salt.
Cover and cook over a medium heat until the harder vegetables are beginning to soften.
Add the courgette and continue cooking until all the vegetables are tender but still hold their shape.
Add the coconut paste and stir gently to coat the vegetables.
Cook for 2–3 minutes over a low heat.
Remove the pan from the heat.
Heat the coconut oil in a small pan until warm.
Add the curry leaves and allow them to sizzle briefly.
Pour the coconut oil and curry leaves over the avial.

learning.did-you-know: Avial is strongly associated with Kerala and is traditionally made with a mixture of vegetables cooked with coconut and yogurt. It is also an important part of a traditional Kerala sadya feast.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to cook a mixture of vegetables so they remain distinct, make a coarse coconut paste and finish a dish with yogurt without allowing it to split.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A distinctive Kerala vegetable dish that shows how coconut and yogurt can create a thick, lightly spiced accompaniment without a conventional curry sauce.
chapman-tips: Cut the vegetables into similar-sized batons so they cook evenly.
Add vegetables according to their cooking time rather than putting everything in at once.
Keep the coconut paste slightly coarse rather than blending it completely smooth.
Don't boil the avial after adding the yogurt or it may split.
Coconut oil is particularly valuable here because its flavour is part of the character of the dish.
The finished dish should be thick enough for the coconut mixture to coat the vegetables rather than form a loose sauce.
notes.batch-cooking: Avial is best fresh because the vegetables and yogurt can lose texture when frozen and reheated. If freezing is necessary, freeze without the yogurt and add fresh yogurt after reheating.
notes.leftovers: Reheat gently and serve with rice. Leftover avial can also be eaten at room temperature as part of a larger meal.
